g.data-narration: Welcome to CUDA Trapezoidal Rule II. We'll learn to optimize the basic atomicAdd approach using shared memory tree reduction.
g.text: CUDA Trapezoidal Rule II: Improving Performance

g.data-narration: The basic CUDA trapezoidal rule uses atomicAdd to sum all thread results into one global variable. This creates a severe performance bottleneck.
g.text: The basic version I uses atomicAdd to accumulate results:
atomicAdd(result, val); // All N threads serialize here!

g.data-narration: atomicAdd is slow because it serializes access: only one thread can update the global variable at a time, causing massive contention.
g.text: 1. Serialization: Only 1 thread at a time

g.data-narration: The solution: reduce locally within each block using shared memory, then combine block results. This replaces N atomic operations with log N steps.
g.text: Replace atomicAdd bottleneck with tree-structured reduction:
O(N) atomic ops → O(log N) + O(numBlocks)

g.data-narration: Shared memory is on-chip, 100x faster than global memory. Each block has its own shared memory accessible by all its threads.
g.text: Scope: Block (threads in same block share)

g.data-narration: Tree reduction: each thread loads its value into shared memory at its thread index. We'll reduce 8 values using 3 steps.
g.text: Each thread loads its value into shared memory at index threadIdx.x:

g.data-narration: Step 1: stride = 4. Threads 0-3 add the value 4 positions ahead. This halves the active threads in parallel.
Step 2: stride = 2. Threads 0-1 add values 2 positions apart. Now we have 2 active threads combining 4 values each.
Step 3: stride = 1. Thread 0 adds the last two values. Now sdata[0] holds the complete sum of all 8 values!
The __syncthreads() barrier is mandatory between reduction steps. It ensures all threads finish before any proceed. Without it, race conditions occur.
g.text: __syncthreads() ensures ALL threads complete before ANY proceed:
⚠️ Must be reached by ALL threads!

g.data-narration: Here's the complete optimized kernel with dynamic shared memory, tree reduction loop, and single block-level write.
g.text: __global__ void trapReduction(double a, double h, int n, double* blockSums) {

g.data-narration: Host code allocates block sums array, launches kernel with shared memory size, then sums the partial results on CPU.
Non-power-of-2 block sizes cause problems: some elements may not be reduced. Solution: always use 128, 256, or 512 threads.
g.text: Problem: With blockDim.x = 7, stride 3 leaves index 6 unreduced!
Extra threads contribute zero (guard: if i <= n)

g.data-narration: Sequential addressing (stride-halving) is preferred: adjacent threads access adjacent memory, avoiding warp divergence and bank conflicts.
